title: Implement spec plan interactive pushback loop
description:
Implement the interactive conversation loop for spec plan. When the planner encounters under-specified requirements, ambiguous verification, or needs user decisions (e.g., which approach for component testing), it presents options and waits for input. The loop continues until all task specs have verification strategies or the user explicitly stops.

PRD ref: The Interactive Pushback Loop (with full example conversation).

## Done when
- [ ] `src/plan/conversation.rs` implements the interactive loop: present findings -> ask questions -> refine specs -> repeat
- [ ] When a task's signal can't be classified, presents specific options to the user (like the PRD example)
- [ ] Supports adding new foundational tasks discovered during conversation
- [ ] Supports updating existing task specs based on user feedback
- [ ] Supports the user saying "stop" or "accept" to finalize current specs
- [ ] Reads user input from stdin, prints to stdout
- [ ] Unit tests with replaying cassettes (simulated user input)
- [ ] `cargo check` passes
- [ ] `cargo test` passes

## Affected files
- src/plan/conversation.rs (new)
- src/plan/mod.rs (modified)
notes:
## Verify

- Expect: Compiles without errors

If PushbackRequired is returned, print the reason and skip that requirement (it will be handled in the conversation loop).

title: Rework speck plan to non-interactive write-and-score mode
description:
Rework `speck plan` to remove the interactive conversation loop. Currently plan runs a multi-turn interactive session where the user answers questions via stdin (src/plan/conversation.rs). Instead, plan should:

1. Take a spec doc (markdown file) as input via `speck plan <path-to-doc>`
2. Run all analysis passes non-interactively (survey, signal classification, reconciliation)
3. Write derived TaskSpecs to `.speck/tasks/` on disk
4. Output to stdout: a summary of derived tasks, structured feedback, questions with enumerated options and recommendations

The conversation loop (ConversationLoop in src/plan/conversation.rs) should be replaced with a single-pass analysis that writes specs and prints feedback. The LLM is still used for survey, classification, and reconciliation — just not for interactive Q&A.

The feedback format must be structured enough for an LLM to act on (the user will use an LLM to update their spec doc based on speck's feedback). Questions should have concrete options, not open-ended prose.

## Done when
- [ ] `speck plan <doc.md>` reads a markdown file and runs non-interactively
- [ ] All analysis passes (survey, classification, reconciliation) execute without user input
- [ ] TaskSpecs are written to `.speck/tasks/` directory
- [ ] Stdout shows: derived task summary, structured feedback, questions with options
- [ ] Feedback questions have enumerated options with a recommended default
- [ ] No stdin reads during plan execution
- [ ] cargo check passes with no new errors
- [ ] cargo test passes (all existing + any new tests)

## Verify
- Run: cargo check --all-targets --all-features
- Run: cargo test --all-targets --all-features
- Expect: all tests pass, plan runs without prompting for input

title: Add idempotent re-plan: update existing specs on re-run
description:
When the user re-runs `speck plan <doc.md>` after updating their spec doc, speck should update existing TaskSpecs on disk rather than creating duplicates. This makes the plan-feedback-update cycle idempotent.

Matching logic: compare newly derived tasks against existing `.speck/tasks/*.yaml` files. Match by semantic similarity of title/requirement (LLM-assisted) or by explicit spec ID if the doc references one. Matched tasks get updated in place (same ID). Unmatched new tasks get new IDs. Tasks on disk that no longer correspond to anything in the doc should be flagged (not silently deleted).

The stdout output should indicate what changed: "Updated SPECK-42 (title changed)", "New task SPECK-45", "Orphaned SPECK-41 (no longer in doc)".

## Done when

- [ ] Existing spec IDs are preserved when tasks match
- [ ] New tasks get new IDs
- [ ] Orphaned tasks (on disk but not in new plan) are reported to stdout
- [ ] Stdout shows diff summary: updated/new/orphaned counts
- [ ] cargo check passes with no new errors

## Verify

- Expect: all tests pass, re-running plan with same doc produces no new files

title: Add plan scoring with specificity and verifiability feedback
description:
Add a 0-100% readiness score to `speck plan` output, evaluating the spec doc on two dimensions the user controls:

- **Specificity** (0-100): Are requirements unambiguous enough for speck to decompose into buildable tasks? Low score = vague language, missing details, multiple interpretations possible.
- **Verifiability** (0-100): Can speck derive concrete acceptance checks from what the user wrote? Low score = no testable criteria, subjective quality, "it should work well."

Overall score = weighted combination of both dimensions.

Output format (stdout):
- Overall score as a single number with a short verdict ("Ready to sync", "Needs refinement", etc.)
- Per-dimension breakdown with specific issues
- Questions: concrete, with 2-4 enumerated options and a recommended answer
- Recommendations: specific edits to the doc that would improve the score

The feedback must be structured enough that an LLM can parse it and apply suggested changes to the spec doc.

## Done when
- [ ] `speck plan <doc.md>` outputs an overall 0-100% score
- [ ] Score breaks down into specificity and verifiability sub-scores
- [ ] Each sub-score cites specific sections of the doc that need improvement
- [ ] Questions have 2-4 concrete options with a recommended default
- [ ] Recommendations are actionable edits, not vague suggestions
- [ ] Score is reproducible (same doc = same score, modulo LLM variance)
- [ ] cargo check passes with no new errors
- [ ] cargo test passes (all existing + any new tests)

## Verify
- Run: cargo test --all-targets --all-features
- Run: cargo check --all-targets --all-features
- Expect: all tests pass

## Affected files
- src/commands/plan.rs (modified — add score output)

title: Derive affected globs during plan via linkage resolution
description:
During `speck plan`, after decomposing requirements into TaskSpecs, resolve each task's abstract module references (in context.modules) to concrete file glob patterns using the codebase map and linkage resolution.

The resolution flow:
1. Load or generate codebase map (already done in survey pass)
2. For each TaskSpec's context.modules entries, use linkage::resolve() to map abstract names to concrete module paths
3. Convert resolved paths to directory-level globs (e.g., "MetricsService" resolves to "src/services/metrics" becomes "src/services/metrics/**")
4. Store the result in the TaskSpec's affected_globs field

Directory-level globs are the right default — they match how module boundaries actually work and provide good scheduling parallelism without over-predicting.

If a module reference can't be resolved (new module that doesn't exist yet), generate a best-effort glob from the module name and flag it in the feedback output.

## Done when
- [ ] Each TaskSpec produced by plan has affected_globs populated
- [ ] Globs are derived from codebase map linkage resolution, not hard-coded
- [ ] Unresolvable module refs produce best-effort globs with a warning in feedback
- [ ] Globs use directory-level patterns (e.g., src/services/metrics/**)
- [ ] cargo check passes with no new errors
- [ ] cargo test passes (all existing + any new tests)

## Verify
- Run: cargo test --all-targets --all-features
- Run: cargo check --all-targets --all-features
- Expect: all tests pass

## Affected files

title: Enrich speck sync to serialize full spec into bead body
description:
Enrich the bead body produced by `speck sync beads` to serialize the full TaskSpec, not just acceptance criteria and dependencies. After sync, the bead in bd must be self-contained — an agent reading the bead body has everything it needs.

The bead body must include:
1. **affected: line** — comma-separated globs from TaskSpec.affected_globs (e.g., "affected: src/services/metrics/**, src/lib.rs"). This is what blacksmith's scheduler reads for conflict detection.
2. **Verification checks** — serialized in a parseable format so `speck validate` can reconstruct them from the bead body. Use a structured markdown section with a machine-readable delimiter (e.g., fenced code block with yaml/json).
3. **Acceptance criteria** — already present, keep as-is
4. **Module context** — which modules are involved and what patterns to follow
5. **Dependencies** — already present, keep as-is

The format must round-trip: `speck validate` must be able to parse the bead body back into enough structure to execute verification checks. Design the body format with this in mind.

Update issue_body() in src/sync/beads.rs to produce the enriched format.

## Done when
- [ ] Bead body includes an `affected:` line with resolved globs
- [ ] Bead body includes verification checks in a parseable format
- [ ] Bead body includes module context section
- [ ] Existing acceptance criteria and dependencies sections preserved
- [ ] Format is parseable by speck validate (round-trip test)
- [ ] cargo check passes with no new errors

title: Make speck validate read spec from bd and output structured JSON
description:

Two modes:
1. `speck validate <spec-id>` — reads from local .speck/tasks/ (existing behavior, keep for pre-sync iteration)
2. `speck validate --bead <bead-id>` — reads the bead body from bd via the IssueTracker port, parses the serialized spec back into a TaskSpec, then runs verification checks

The bd-reading mode parses the enriched bead body format produced by speck-159 to reconstruct the verification checks.

Output changes:
- Exit code 0 = all executable checks pass
- Exit code 1 = at least one check failed
- Add `--json` flag for structured output: JSON object with per-check results (name, passed, detail, category)
- Human-readable output remains the default (existing format)

This is the contract blacksmith will call: `speck validate --bead <bead-id> --json`

The speck validate command runs in the worktree context so it can execute checks against the agent's code. Pass the bead ID from the worker assignment.

title: Add retry and human escalation for speck validation failures
description:
[BLACKSMITH REPO] When speck validation fails during pre-integration, blacksmith should retry the agent (re-spawn a coding session with the validation failure details in the prompt) up to N times. After N failures, escalate to human review.

This extends the existing circuit breaker pattern in blacksmith (src/integrator.rs CircuitBreaker) to cover speck validation failures, not just compiler/merge failures.

Retry flow:
1. Agent finishes coding
2. speck validate fails (e.g., acceptance check not met)
3. Blacksmith re-spawns agent with prompt: "Previous attempt failed speck validation: <failure details>. Fix the issues and try again."
4. Repeat up to N times (configurable, default 2)
5. After N failures: mark bead as needs-human-review, log details, skip to next work

The retry count should be separate from the existing integration circuit breaker (which covers merge/compiler failures). A task could fail validation 2 times and then also need 1 integration fix attempt — these are independent counters.

## Done when
- [ ] Validation failure triggers agent retry with failure context in prompt
- [ ] Retry limit is configurable (default 2)
- [ ] After max retries, bead is flagged for human review (not silently dropped)
- [ ] Retry counter is independent from integration circuit breaker
- [ ] Failure details from speck validate JSON are included in retry prompt
- [ ] cargo check passes with no new errors
- [ ] cargo test passes (all existing + any new tests)

## Verify

title: Add cost-per-session ceiling to metrics targets
design: Current metrics.targets only checks cost data is logged (pct_sessions >= 99%), not actual cost values. Sessions routinely exceed $2 (session 6: $3.84, session 11: $2.27) with no alert. Add a new [[metrics.targets.rules]] entry in .blacksmith/config.toml: kind=cost.estimate_usd, compare=max, threshold=2.0, direction=below, label=Cost per session. This gives early warning when sessions are burning excessive budget.

title: Add turn-budget bead guard to PROMPT.md
design: speck-150 consumed 215 turns across 2 sessions with no early abort signal. Add a rule to PROMPT.md under Turn Budget: If a bead exceeds 60 turns without reaching verification phase, STOP. Run bd update with status=open and notes=[TOO-LARGE] then exit. The coordinator will route it to the break-down-issue skill. Prevents runaway sessions from burning budget on oversized beads.

title: Increase analysis cadence from every-1 to every-3 sessions
design: Change .blacksmith/config.toml [improvements] analyze_every from 1 to 3. At $0.78 per analysis run, every-session analysis adds significant overhead. Every-3 still captures trends while cutting analysis cost by 67%.

title: Ban Explore subagent in analysis prompt
design: The analysis session (session 12) launched a Task(subagent_type=Explore) to find well-known files (.blacksmith/config.toml, PROMPT.md, .beads/issues.jsonl). This violates PROMPT.md Rule and costs ~$0.14 in Haiku tokens for no benefit. Fix: In the blacksmith analysis prompt template (the prompt injected for self-improvement sessions), add explicit instruction: 'Read files directly — do NOT use Task with subagent_type Explore. File paths: .blacksmith/config.toml, PROMPT.md, .beads/issues.jsonl, Cargo.toml.' This makes the analysis session cheaper and faster.

title: Fix bead completion tracking in metrics
design: blacksmith metrics beads shows 0/10 beads closed despite issues.jsonl having 20+ closed beads. Root cause: blacksmith session runner does not capture bead close events from bd update, so the metrics bead report always shows open. Fix: (1) In the blacksmith session runner, after a session exits with code 0, check if the assigned bead was closed via bd show and record a bead.closed event. (2) Alternatively, add a post-session hook in config.toml that runs bd show and feeds the status into metrics ingestion. Files to change: blacksmith src (session runner or post-session hook logic), .blacksmith/config.toml (if hook-based approach).

title: Add terse output directive to analysis prompt
design: Output tokens are 73% of analysis session cost ($0.67 of $0.92 in session 14, 8950 tokens at $75/MTok). The analysis prompt template in blacksmith coordinator (src/coordinator.rs or wherever the analysis prompt is assembled) should prepend: 'Be extremely concise. Output only data tables, scores, and bd/blacksmith commands. Do not explain reasoning at length.' This reduces output tokens by 30-50%, saving ~$0.20-0.35 per analysis session.

title: Use cheaper model for analysis sessions
design: Analysis sessions read metrics and file beads — they don't need Opus-level reasoning. Add a model override to blacksmith config (e.g. [analysis] model = 'sonnet') and pass --model to the claude agent args when spawning analysis. Opus output tokens cost $75/MTok vs Sonnet $24/MTok. Session 14 output tokens: 8950 → Opus $0.67 vs Sonnet $0.21, a 68% reduction. Files: src/coordinator.rs (analysis spawn logic), .blacksmith/config.toml (new [analysis] section).

title: Filter analysis sessions from analysis metrics window
design: Analysis sessions are analyzing other analysis sessions, creating a recursive cost chain (sessions 12-15 are four consecutive analysis runs). The analysis prompt builder should filter out sessions whose result contains 'Analysis Complete' before substituting into recent_metrics. Change: in blacksmith coordinator code where recent_metrics is assembled, add a filter that excludes sessions tagged as analysis runs. This prevents the agent from wasting turns on self-referential data and focuses it on actual work sessions.

title: Skip analysis when improvement backlog exceeds threshold
design: With 14 open improvements and 6 open chore beads, analysis sessions keep discovering the same issues and skipping them - burning cost with no new value. Add a backlog gate: if open improvement count > 10, skip spawning analysis sessions until the backlog is cleared. Change: in blacksmith coordinator analysis trigger logic, before spawning an analysis agent, count open improvements via 'blacksmith improve list' and skip if count exceeds threshold. Config: add improvements.backlog_threshold = 10 to config.toml.

title: Auto-decompose beads stuck after 2 session failures
description: When a bead exceeds 2 consecutive session attempts without closing, the orchestrator should auto-invoke /break-down-issue to decompose it into smaller sub-beads, then pause the original. Currently speck-150 has consumed 3 sessions and 257 turns (32% of all turns) with no closure. Immediate implementation: add a PROMPT.md instruction — Before starting a bead, check blacksmith metrics beads. If the current bead has 2+ prior sessions, invoke /break-down-issue to decompose it instead of retrying. Longer term: add stuck_threshold config to blacksmith config.toml. Files to change: .blacksmith/PROMPT.md (add stuck-bead check instruction).

title: Add single-script analysis directive to ANALYSIS_PROMPT.md
design: Analysis sessions make 30+ sequential Bash calls to parse session JSONL files, each requiring a round-trip. Add a directive to .blacksmith/ANALYSIS_PROMPT.md: 'Extract all session metrics in a SINGLE comprehensive Python script passed to Bash. Do NOT make sequential Bash calls for data extraction.' This eliminates ~30 turns of overhead per analysis session, cutting cost by ~40%.

title: Add no-exploration directive to ANALYSIS_PROMPT.md
design: Analysis sessions ignore pre-computed metrics injected via {{recent_metrics}} and spend turns exploring the filesystem. Add directive: 'All session metrics you need are provided above. Do NOT explore the filesystem, read session files, or use Explore subagents. Start analysis immediately from the provided data.' Prevents 10+ wasted exploration turns per analysis session.

title: Prioritize process chores over new improvement filing
design: 8 open P0 process chores and 18 improvements exist, none implemented. Analysis sessions keep filing new improvements without throughput. Change blacksmith scheduler to prioritize open chore-type beads when process backlog exceeds 5 items. This ensures filed improvements get implemented rather than accumulating indefinitely.

Implement the codebase cartography tool that generates a structural snapshot of the target project. The map includes directory structure, module boundaries, key interfaces, and test infrastructure. Tied to a commit hash, treated as a derived artifact.

PRD ref: Two-Pass Planning — Pass 1. Storage Architecture — Codebase map lifecycle. Build Path step 5.

## Done when
- [ ] src/map/mod.rs defines CodebaseMap struct with fields: commit_hash, generated_at, modules (list of module summaries), directory_tree, test_infrastructure
- [ ] src/map/generator.rs implements map generation: walks project files via ctx.fs, identifies module boundaries (directories with mod.rs or lib.rs), lists key interfaces
- [ ] src/map/mod.rs defines ModuleSummary struct with: path, public_items (functions, structs, traits), dependencies
- [ ] Map is serialized as YAML to .spec-cache/codebase_map.yaml
- [ ] Map includes the commit hash it was generated against (via ctx.git)
- [ ] All I/O goes through ServiceContext ports
- [ ] Unit test with replaying cassette: generate a map from a known project structure
- [ ] cargo check passes
- [ ] cargo test passes

## Verify
- Run: cargo test map
- Expect: Map generation tests pass, produces valid YAML output

## Affected files
- src/map/mod.rs (modified)
- src/map/generator.rs (new)
notes: ## Verify\n- Run: cargo check --release\n- Expect: Compiles cleanly
close_reason: Implement codebase map generation (already complete)

title: Implement LLM client live adapter with Claude API

Replace the stub LLM live adapter with a real implementation that calls the Claude API (Anthropic messages API). Uses reqwest for HTTP. API key read via ANTHROPIC_API_KEY env var. This unblocks all LLM-dependent features (spec plan).

PRD ref: Port Inventory — LlmClient.

## Done when

- [ ] Properly maps CompletionRequest to Anthropic API request format
- [ ] Properly maps Anthropic API response to CompletionResponse
- [ ] Returns meaningful error on missing API key, network failure, API error
- [ ] Add reqwest and tokio to Cargo.toml dependencies
- [ ] `cargo check` passes
- [ ] `cargo test` passes (existing tests unaffected — they use replaying adapters)

title: Define task spec YAML schema and Rust types
description:
Define the Rust types for the task spec format and ensure they round-trip through YAML. This is the contract that spec plan produces and spec validate consumes. Includes signal type classification, verification strategies, and abstract codebase references.

PRD ref: Task Spec Format (Draft) section — all three YAML examples.

## Done when
- [ ] `src/spec/mod.rs` exists with module declarations

- [ ] `src/spec/signal.rs` defines SignalType enum: Clear, Fuzzy, InternalLogic
- [ ] `src/spec/verification.rs` defines VerificationStrategy enum with variants: DirectAssertion, RefactorToExpose, TraceAssertion — each with their specific fields
- [ ] `src/spec/check.rs` defines VerificationCheck enum with variants: TestSuite, SqlAssertion, CommandOutput, MigrationRollback, Custom
- [ ] All types derive Serialize + Deserialize
- [ ] Unit test: the three YAML examples from the PRD parse into the Rust types correctly
- [ ] Unit test: round-trip (serialize then deserialize) produces identical structs
- [ ] `cargo check` passes
- [ ] `cargo test` passes

- src/spec/task_spec.rs (new)
- src/spec/signal.rs (new)
- src/spec/verification.rs (new)
- src/spec/check.rs (new)
- src/lib.rs (modified — add spec module)

- [ ] `CassetteConfig::panic_on_unspecified()` returns a config where all paths are None (ports panic if called without a cassette)

title: Implement spec validate core: load spec and run checks
description:
Implement the core validation engine that loads a task spec from the store and runs its verification checks. This is the exit gate — it proves work is done. Supports test_suite checks (run a command, assert exit code), command_output checks (run command, assert on stdout), and abstract reference resolution against the codebase.

PRD ref: The Core Idea — Validate. Build Path step 3.

- [ ] `src/validate/reference.rs` resolves abstract module references against the codebase (checks files exist via ctx.fs)
- [ ] Returns a ValidationResult with per-check pass/fail and overall status
- [ ] Prints a human-readable report of results

title: Implement linkage resolution and drift detection
description:
Implement the linkage layer that resolves abstract spec references against the current codebase map and detects drift. When a spec references "MetricsService" abstractly, linkage resolves it to the actual file path. When that file changes after the spec was written, drift detection warns.

PRD ref: Storage Architecture — Linkage lifecycle. Three Separate Lifecycles.

## Done when
- [ ] \`src/linkage/mod.rs\` defines the linkage resolution engine
- [ ] \`resolve(ctx, spec, codebase_map)\` maps abstract module references to concrete file paths
- [ ] \`detect_drift(ctx, spec, old_map, new_map)\` identifies specs whose referenced modules have changed
- [ ] Drift report shows: which specs are affected, what changed, whether re-planning is recommended
- [ ] Integrated into \`speck validate\` — validation warns on drift before running checks
- [ ] All I/O through ServiceContext ports
- [ ] Unit tests with replaying cassettes
- [ ] \`cargo check\` passes
- [ ] \`cargo test\` passes

## Affected files
- src/linkage/mod.rs (new)
- src/linkage/resolve.rs (new)
- src/linkage/drift.rs (new)
- src/lib.rs (modified — add linkage module)
- src/validate/mod.rs (modified — integrate drift check)
close_reason: Implement linkage resolution and drift detection

title: Implement recursive feedback: validation failure flows back to plan
description:
When spec validate fails, the failure information should be structured and available for spec plan to consume. This closes the feedback loop: either the implementation needs fixing, or the spec itself was flawed and plan should revise the contract.

PRD ref: The Recursive Feedback Property.

## Done when
- [ ] ValidationResult includes structured failure details: which checks failed, actual vs expected, failure context
- [ ] \`src/plan/feedback.rs\` can ingest a ValidationResult and propose spec revisions
- [ ] Distinguishes between implementation failures (agent needs to iterate) and spec flaws (plan revises contract)
- [ ] \`speck validate\` output includes actionable next steps based on failure type
- [ ] Unit tests with replaying cassettes: validation failure triggers correct feedback classification
- [ ] \`cargo check\` passes
- [ ] \`cargo test\` passes

## Verify
- Run: \cargo test feedback\
- Expect: All feedback loop tests pass

## Affected files

- src/plan/mod.rs (modified)
- src/validate/mod.rs (modified — enrich ValidationResult)

## Verify

- Expect: clean compilation
